---
date: "2026-06-22"
---

## The Global Polynomial Trap

- Part 8 — the global high-order polynomial trap to avoid

::: notes
Welcome back to regression discontinuity, part eight of seventeen. In part seven we saw fuzzy R-D is just instrumental variables. Now: how do you actually estimate the height of the outcome curve on each side of the cutoff? This is where a generation of empiricists made a mistake you should learn to avoid on sight. We meet the trap.
:::

## The Tempting Trap: A Global Polynomial

- Regress $Y$ on $D$ plus $X, X^2, X^3, X^4$ over the whole sample
- Let slopes differ each side; read the coefficient on $D$ as the jump
- Seems principled: flexible, uses all data, one tidy regression
- For years, RD papers did exactly this
- Gelman and Imbens (2019) named the trap precisely

::: notes
The tempting move is to fit one global polynomial to all the data — say, regress the outcome Y on a treatment dummy D plus X, X-squared, X-cubed, and X-to-the-fourth over the entire sample, letting the slopes differ on each side of the cutoff, and read the coefficient on D as the jump. It seems principled: a high-order polynomial is flexible, it uses all the data efficiently, and it gives you a tidy single regression. For years, R-D papers did exactly this. It is a trap, and Gelman and Imbens, in two thousand nineteen, named the trap precisely. The full citation: Gelman and Imbens, Why High-Order Polynomials Should Not Be Used in Regression Discontinuity Designs, in the Journal of Business and Economic Statistics.
:::

## Why Global Polynomials Mislead

- Polynomials are *erratic near the edges* of the data
- Distant points — Apple, a micro-cap — dictate curvature at $c$
- The estimate swings wildly between a cubic and a quartic
- Implied weights on observations can be bizarre, even *negative*
- A sign the estimator does what you'd never endorse

::: notes
High-order global polynomials produce noisy, misleading estimates of the value at a boundary point, for three concrete reasons. First, polynomials are notoriously erratic near the edges of the data — and the cutoff, by construction, is the pivotal point where you are reading two one-sided extrapolations that meet. Second, a global polynomial lets data far from the cutoff — Apple, on the Russell one thousand side; a tiny micro-cap deep in the two thousand — dictate the curvature right at the cutoff. Those distant points have nothing to do with the marginal stocks you care about, yet a fourth-order polynomial fit to the whole range will twist itself to accommodate them and, in doing so, can manufacture a spurious wiggle, or erase a real jump, exactly at the boundary. Third, the estimate is disturbingly sensitive to the arbitrary choice of polynomial order: the jump you report can swing wildly between a cubic and a quartic, with no principled way to pick. Gelman and Imbens showed the implicit weights such a polynomial places on individual observations can be bizarre, even negative — a sign the estimator is doing something you would never endorse if you saw it spelled out.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Fitting one **global high-order polynomial** to all the data looks principled but is a trap: it is erratic at the edges, lets *distant* data dictate the curvature at $c$, and swings wildly with the arbitrary polynomial order (Gelman–Imbens 2019). Next: the fix — local linear regression.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part eight: to estimate the jump, you must estimate the height of the outcome curve on each side of the cutoff. The tempting trap is a global high-order polynomial fit to all the data — flexible, efficient-looking, one tidy regression. But Gelman and Imbens showed it produces noisy, misleading boundary estimates: polynomials are erratic at the edges, distant data dictate the curvature at the cutoff, and the answer is hostage to the arbitrary polynomial order, with implied weights that can be bizarre or even negative. In part nine we meet the fix — local linear regression with a triangular kernel.
:::
